title: National policies for orderly, safe, regular, and responsible migration
subtitle: The United Nations measures whether national migration policies meet International Organization for Migration criteria in a variety of policy domains, to ensure orderly, safe, regular, and responsible migration.
caption:
National policies for orderly, safe, regular, and responsible migration, 2021
The United Nations measures whether national migration policies meet International Organization for Migration criteria in a variety of policy domains, to ensure orderly, safe, regular, and responsible migration.

The choropleth map displays the status of national migration policies across the globe in 2021, categorizing countries based on the extent to which they meet International Organization for Migration criteria. The visualization utilizes a five-step color scale ranging from deep red, representing nations that require further progress, through orange for those partially meeting criteria, light blue for those meeting criteria, and dark blue for nations that fully meet criteria, with hatched grey areas indicating no available data. The most prevalent category visible on the map is "Meets criteria," shown in light blue, covering the majority of North America, Europe, Russia, Australia, and large portions of South America and Africa. In contrast, the "Partially meets criteria" category, colored orange, appears in scattered clusters including China, India, Brazil, and several nations in North Africa and the Middle East. A smaller number of countries fall into the "Fully meets criteria" band, depicted in dark blue, with visible examples including Peru, South Africa, and Iran. The lowest performance tier, "Requires further progress," is marked in red and is visible in specific locations such as Yemen, Sudan, and Myanmar. Significant portions of the map, particularly in central Africa and parts of the Middle East, feature hatched patterns indicating that no data was reported for those jurisdictions. The geographic distribution shows a concentration of higher compliance in the Global North and parts of South America, while lower compliance and data gaps are more frequent across Africa and parts of Asia.
